issues.title: Jimmy Clay & Roy Kizer Golf Course Rezoning Initiation
issues.summary: Resolution initiating rezoning of approximately 445 acres of City-owned land (Jimmy Clay and Roy Kizer golf courses) at 5400 Jimmy Clay Drive. Sponsored by CMs Fuentes, Alter, Ellis, and Laine. This could be one of the largest single housing development opportunities in Austin if the land is eventually rezoned for residential/mixed-use.

public_comment.instructions: This was an initiation resolution — the actual rezoning process will have extensive public hearings. Monitor for future hearing dates.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: 445 acres of potential housing on city-owned land is a massive pro-development opportunity. Aligns directly with your YIMBY values. The initiation vote signals council intent; the actual rezoning fights will come later and will need organized pro-housing voices.

issues.title: Convention Center Redevelopment — Petition Rejected by Court
issues.summary: UPDATE (Feb 2026): Travis County District Judge Jessica Mangrum sided with Austin and upheld the City Clerk's rejection of the Austin United PAC petition to halt the $1.6 billion convention center redevelopment. The PAC had collected 25,000+ signatures but the Clerk's office certified it as ~500 signatures short. After a two-day trial in late January, the court ruled for the city. Mayor Watson called it 'a good win for Austinites.' The PAC has announced plans to organize a new petition targeting the November 2026 ballot. Construction on the new complex continues — expected to open by late 2028 with ~620,000 sq ft of rentable space (70% increase over the old center).

public_comment.instructions: Petition was rejected by court. PAC plans new petition for November ballot. Monitor for new signature-gathering efforts.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: The court ruling clears the way for the convention center project to proceed — a win for pro-development interests. However, Austin United PAC is regrouping for a November ballot push, so this could resurface. For now, this drops to background monitoring. The anti-development coalition (SOS Alliance backing) is worth tracking as it overlaps with NIMBY interests on housing.

issues.title: City Council District 1 — Open Seat Election
issues.summary:
UPDATE (April 21, 2026): The District 1 field has grown to at least 8 candidates. CM Natasha Harper-Madison is term-limited, creating the first open seat in 8 years. Twenty candidates total have filed across all five open council seats (Districts 1, 3, 5, 8, 9).

Declared District 1 candidates include:

• Steven Brown, 41 — Medtronic clinical specialist. Former co-chair of Save Austin Now alongside Matt Mackowiak (Travis County Republican Party chair). Anti-developer framing. Residency concern: homestead exemption outside the district. Has raised ~$6,000.

• Alexandria Anderson, 39 — Retired pro sprinter, MLK Neighborhood Association president. Homeowner in District 1 since 2014. No fundraising reported.

• Naguib 'Simon' Nahas — Housing affordability advocate who has 'worked for years trying to lower rents in Austin.' Supports smaller minimum lot sizes and more housing. Notable: would be the first White council member from District 1 since geographic representation began in 2014 — the seat has historically been held by the city's only Black council member.

• Misael Daguan Ramos, 39 — Ran for the District 1 seat in 2022, giving him name recognition and campaign infrastructure advantage over first-time candidates.

• Kyra Lorena Rogers, 31 — Lives in an apartment off Old Manor Road in District 1. Notably was not yet a registered voter when she filed.

• NEW: Amber Karessa Goodwin, 44 — Assistant district attorney at the Travis County DA's Office. Home address verified in District 1. Brings prosecutorial and justice system experience to the field.

• At least 2 additional candidates have appointed treasurers.

Filing period: July 20 – August 17, 2026.

**May 17 update:** The D1 open seat continues to attract candidates ahead of the July 20-August 17 filing period. At least 8 candidates declared so far. Early campaign finance reports show Steven Brown with ~$6,000 raised — modest by council race standards. The November 3 election will also include Districts 3, 5, 8, and 9. Twenty candidates total have filed across all five open seats. Key evaluation window: watch for candidates' positions on the Density Bonus vote (expected May 28 — 9 DAYS), the BEATF bond proposal, and Project Connect — these three items will define the pro-building litmus test for the next council. ⚡ The Pct 4 runoff early voting is NOW OPEN (through May 22, Election Day May 26 — 6 DAYS) — this is also a moment to gauge candidate activity and organizing capacity across the broader Austin political landscape.

public_comment.instructions: Monitor candidate announcements. Filing opens July 20. Evaluate housing/development positions carefully. Consider early engagement or donation to most-aligned candidate.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: This is YOUR district's council seat and it's open for the first time in 8 years. The field is growing fast — at least 8 candidates now, including new entrant Amber Goodwin (assistant DA). Simon Nahas is the most explicitly pro-housing candidate so far, advocating for smaller lot sizes and more building. Brown's Save Austin Now connection remains a red flag for YIMBY priorities. Goodwin's prosecutorial background could appeal to public safety voters. The racial dynamics of the seat (historically held by the city's only Black council member) add complexity. With filing not closing until August, evaluate candidates' housing/development positions carefully. The DBC council vote is May 28 — 9 DAYS AWAY — a key litmus test. This is the single most important local race for you in 2026.

issues.title: Commissioner Precinct 4 — Democratic Runoff: Morales vs Ledesma-Woody
issues.summary:
UPDATE (March 3 primary results): George Morales III (40.17%, 9,445 votes) and Susanna Ledesma-Woody (35.18%, 8,273 votes) advance to the May 26 runoff. Ofelia Maldonado Zapata (18.75%) and Gavino Fernandez Jr. (5.9%) were eliminated. Margaret Gómez is retiring after holding this seat since 1995. No Republican is running — the runoff winner takes the seat.

• George Morales III — Former elected Constable Precinct 4, current constable deputy. 20 years with Travis County government, including 16 years as an administrator on Commissioners Court focused on budgeting, housing, and community access.

• Susanna Ledesma-Woody — Project manager at AMD, 14-year Del Valle ISD school board trustee, founding member of Del Valle Community Coalition. Raised in Del Valle, has framed the race as 'policy and governance experience vs. policing experience.' This is her third run for this seat.

**May 17 update:** ⚡ EARLY VOTING STARTS TOMORROW (May 18-22). The runoff is May 26 — just 9 DAYS AWAY. KUT published a comprehensive Travis County voter guide for the May 26 runoff (May 15). Community Impact published detailed candidate Q&As on May 14. The race has crystallized around the contrast between Ledesma-Woody's policy and governance experience (14 years as Del Valle ISD trustee, AMD project manager, founding member of Del Valle Community Coalition) and Morales's policing background (former Precinct 4 Constable). LIV endorsed Ledesma-Woody, criticizing Morales's 'it's my turn' framing. Since no Republican is running, the winner will be the next commissioner — the first new Precinct 4 commissioner in over 30 years. Turnout will be extremely low (8-12%) — individual organizing effort has outsized impact. EARLY VOTING LOCATIONS: Any polling location in Travis County. Vote at votetexas.gov for location finder.

**May 19 update:** ⚡ EARLY VOTING IS NOW OPEN (through May 22). The runoff is May 26 — 6 DAYS AWAY. KUT published a comprehensive profile of both candidates on May 18. Key details: Morales served as Pct 4 Constable for nearly 10 years before stepping down to run; during COVID, his office distributed 380,000 vaccines. Ledesma-Woody has been a Del Valle ISD trustee since 2011 and was the only elected official to vote against the Tesla tax incentive deal. Austin Chronicle published endorsement recommendations for the May 26 runoff. With early voting now underway and turnout expected at 8-12%, every individual vote and organizing effort has outsized impact.

public_comment.instructions: Verify voter registration at votetexas.gov. Research both candidates' positions on development, transit, and infrastructure. Consider volunteering — small canvassing effort can shift outcomes in low-turnout runoffs.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: The primary narrowed the field to two candidates with very different backgrounds — a law enforcement veteran (Morales) vs. a policy/governance professional (Ledesma-Woody). LIV (League of Independent Voters) has endorsed Ledesma-Woody, calling out Morales's 'it's my turn' framing. Runoff turnout will be extremely low (8-12%), making your individual vote and any organizing effort outsized in impact. The commissioner directly influences county-level land use, transportation infrastructure, and budget decisions. 6 DAYS until the May 26 runoff. ⚡ EARLY VOTING IS NOW OPEN (through May 22) — this is the highest-leverage action you can take this week. Vote early, then spend the remaining days helping others get to the polls.

issues.title: Springdale Farm Zoning Follow-Up — District 3
issues.summary: Follow-up staff report on the Springdale Farm zoning case (C14-2026-0004) in District 3, near East Austin. A neighborhood postponement request was filed. This is an example of the neighborhood vs. development tension playing out at the parcel level. The Planning Commission heard this item on March 25 — monitor for outcome and next steps.

public_comment.instructions: Planning Commission meetings allow public comment. Check austintexas.gov for sign-up details.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Adjacent to your area. The neighborhood postponement tactic is a classic NIMBY delay strategy worth monitoring. Each individual case sets precedent for how similar cases in your neighborhood will be handled.

issues.title: Austin Housing Finance Corp — $5.5M Loan Authorization
issues.summary: Council authorized a $5.5 million AHFC loan for affordable housing. The council recesses its regular meeting to convene as the AHFC Board of Directors for these items.
issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Aligned with your housing values. Low contestedness means your engagement isn't needed here, but worth tracking as part of the city's overall housing investment picture.

issues.title: Raising Travis County — $5M Childcare Investment
issues.summary: Transfer of $5,046,004 from Raising Travis County fund to support interlocal agreement with City of Austin for childcare quality improvement and out-of-school time capacity, plus $80K for communications consulting.

public_comment.instructions: Register at traviscotx.portal.civicclerk.com

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Lower on your personal salience list but represents good governance — evidence-based investment in childcare infrastructure. The kind of pragmatic spending you'd support.

issues.title: Homelessness Strategic Plan Adopted — 650 New Shelter Beds Planned
issues.summary:
⚡ UPDATE (May 7, 2026): HSO Director David Gray presented the expanded encampment sweep plan to Council at the MAY 5 WORK SESSION. Council received briefings on both the sweeps expansion and operational modifications within Homeless Strategies and Operations. Council also discussed a resolution directing the City Manager to postpone departmental reorganizations associated with the 'One ATS' initiative until council is presented with sufficient information.

May 7: Council APPROVED $497,299 in one-time funding for Integral Care's Homeless Health and Wellness Center program — an amendment to the existing interlocal agreement expanding crisis health services for people experiencing homelessness.

BACKGROUND: Austin City Council approved the AHSO's first-ever strategic plan (2025-2027) on March 26. Key elements: 650 new shelter beds, 2 new housing navigation centers, $101M in proposed additional funding. Director David Gray noted ~6,500 people are currently living on the streets — 'one shelter bed for every five people sleeping unhoused.' Endeavors has taken over operation of downtown shelters (ARCH, Eighth Street) under emergency contract through September 2026.

April 13: AHSO launched the operator solicitation for the South Austin Housing Navigation Center — the city's FIRST city-owned navigation center. Up to $250,000 in funding is available. The center is expected to open late summer or early fall 2026.

⚡ SWEEPS EXPANSION: The plan details 42 city staff across six full-time Homeless Encampment Management (HEM) teams — three geographic teams (North, South, Central Austin) plus teams for transportation corridors, waterways, and litter cleanup. Each team includes two APD officers. CRITICAL CHANGE: outreach workers will NO LONGER be embedded in sweep teams. VOCAL-TX and other advocates are pushing back hard. A series of sweeps last fall cost over $800,000 without leading many people out of homelessness. City leaders acknowledge there aren't enough shelters for everyone displaced.

The $101M funding ask will compete with other priorities in FY2027 budget hearings. The $766.5M BEATF bond proposal (May 6) includes public facilities funding that could support a purpose-built shelter to replace the Marshalling Yard.

**May 17 update:** HSO briefed council on operational modifications. Encampment management operations have expanded from three to five days per week as of mid-May, using a six-step process (prioritization, outreach, 72-hour notice, closure). The city now has 400 more shelter beds than two years ago and has added over 1,100 permanent supportive housing units in the past three years, though only 45 shelter beds are currently open. Austin's local housing voucher program supporting 380+ chronically homeless residents faces cuts amid the $26.4M citywide budget deficit. The South Austin Housing Navigation Center operator solicitation (April 13) closes soon — this will be the city's FIRST city-owned navigation center, expected to open late summer/early fall 2026. The expanded sweeps are now operational five days per week — monitor for early data on outcomes and community impact.

public_comment.instructions: The plan has been adopted. Budget hearings for FY2027 will determine how much of the $101M ask is funded. Monitor council budget sessions.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: The sweeps expansion was presented to council at the May 5 work session. The $497K Integral Care funding approved May 7 is a positive step — expanding crisis health services for people experiencing homelessness. But the fundamental tension remains: removing embedded outreach workers from sweep teams signals a pivot toward enforcement without the services linkage that made HEAL defensible. BUDGET CRUNCH: Prop Q's defeat last November removed $110M that would have funded homelessness services and parks. The FY2027 budget forecast projects $16.8M in social services cuts with no civilian pay raises — the $101M HSO funding ask faces steep headwinds. The $766.5M BEATF bond proposal (May 6) includes public facilities funding that could support shelter infrastructure. Watch the FY2027 budget hearings closely.

issues.title: APD Staffing Audit — No Long-Term Recruitment Plan Found
issues.summary:
UPDATE (April 23, 2026): APD staffing shows incremental progress but vacancy rate remains stubbornly high. Key numbers: 340 vacancies out of 1,816 authorized positions (19% vacancy rate). APD's FY2026 budget is $540M (36.2% of the city's $1.5B general fund). A new $218M contract with the Austin Police Association provides a 28% pay raise over 5 years.

Recent cadet class progress: Three classes have graduated since last spring with a total of 122 new officers. The 156th cadet class, currently comprising 51 cadets, is set to graduate in August 2026. ⚡ The 157th cadet class began April 21 with 65 cadets — the department is scaling toward its goal of 100-person classes. APA President Bullock maintains the department is 'still about 700 officers short' when accounting for population growth.

Some positive signals: attrition has slowed (APD gained 3 officers net in 2025 — first positive year in 5-6 years), and recruitment interest is up 166%. Chief Davis says APD could be fully staffed by end of 2027.

NEW (April 2026): For the first time since August 2023, APD met its target response time of 10 minutes 44 seconds or better for Priority 0 and Priority 1 calls. Additionally, Integral Care (Travis County's mental health authority) is hiring 23 more mental health specialists to respond to crisis calls 24/7 — a significant expansion of non-police crisis response capacity. These are concrete steps toward the 'both/and' model: better police response times AND expanded crisis teams handling calls that don't require patrol officers.

public_comment.instructions: Monitor APD staffing reports at council. Budget hearings for FY2027 will address whether the $540M allocation is sufficient.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: APD staffing directly affects response times and public safety across Austin. The audit's finding that there's no recruitment plan despite 340 vacancies is an accountability failure worth tracking. The $218M contract and 28% pay raise are significant public investments — monitor whether they actually improve staffing. This connects to the broader 'safety is infrastructure' framework: adequate policing plus non-police crisis response (HOST teams) are both needed. The FY2027 budget will be the next decision point.

issues.title: HOME Initiative Delivering Results — Austin Rents Down 16%, Steepest Drop in US
issues.summary:
UPDATE (March 2026): A Pew Charitable Trusts report (March 18, 2026) confirms that Austin's housing construction surge — driven by the HOME Initiative and other pro-building policies — produced the steepest rent decline of any large U.S. city from 2021 to 2026. Key numbers:

• From 2015-2024, Austin added 120,000 units to its housing stock — a 30% increase, more than 3x the national rate (9%).
• Austin's median rent fell from $1,546 (Dec 2021) to $1,296 (Jan 2026) — a 16% decline.
• Austin's median rent is now 4% BELOW the U.S. median ($1,353) after being 15% above it in 2021.
• The combination of strong demand and proactive policy changes (HOME Phase 1 adopted Dec 2023, Phase 2 adopted May 2024) spurred the supply surge.

HOME Initiative implementation milestones: Phase 1 applications accepted Feb 2024, Phase 2 applications accepted Aug-Nov 2024. The initiative now permits up to 3 units on many single-family lots and reduced minimum lot sizes to 1,800 sq ft. Compatibility standards were also reformed — enforcement zones reduced from 540 feet to 75 feet, allowing more height flexibility near single-family homes.

This is concrete, measurable evidence that building more housing lowers costs. Austin is now the national proof point for YIMBY policy.

public_comment.instructions: The HOME Initiative is adopted and in implementation phase. Monitor for missing middle/mixed-use zoning reforms advancing through council in 2026-2027.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: This is the scoreboard. Austin's 16% rent decline — the steepest in any large US city — is direct evidence that the YIMBY policy agenda works. Building more housing lowers costs. This data is your most powerful argument in every future rezoning fight, council race, and public hearing. The Pew report gives this national credibility. Share it. Cite it. Use it to counter every 'building doesn't help' argument you'll hear in the golf course rezoning and council election debates.

issues.title: Missing Middle & Mixed-Use Zoning Expansion — Advancing Through Council
issues.summary:
UPDATE (April 26, 2026): Austin City Council advanced the expansion of missing middle residential and mixed-use zoning on March 26, building on the HOME Initiative. The proposal creates two new zoning categories — one for multiunit 'missing middle' housing (townhomes, cottage courts, single-stair buildings) and one for neighborhood-scale mixed-use (retail on ground floor, housing above).

A January 2026 city zoning study recommended allowing 4-6 units on lots of at least 5,750 square feet. Over the past 40 years, less than 1% of housing built in Austin has been 'missing middle' — the city's 1984 land development code largely supports only single-family homes or large apartment complexes.

⚡ DBC UPDATE (May 8): Council received a WORK SESSION BRIEFING on the citywide Density Bonus program (DBC) on May 5. The DBC has five tiers adding 0-60 feet of extra height in multifamily and commercial zones in exchange for affordable housing. Central affordability proposal: 10% of units affordable at each tier — ownership at 80% MFI, rental at 50% MFI. The DBC introduces two new 'combining districts' offering developers height bonuses of roughly 400 feet or 850 feet above base entitlements — maximum heights up to 750 feet under the lower tier and 1,200 feet under the higher one, a dramatic jump from the current 350-foot cap. Developers face stricter parking caps and must meet design standards (7 for smaller bonus, 10 for larger) while providing on-site affordable housing or paying fees in lieu. Planning Commission hearing HELD April 28; ⚡ COUNCIL VOTE EXPECTED MAY 28 (9 DAYS). This responds to SB 840 (state 'residential in commercial' law) and replaces the controversial prior density bonus program.

Additionally, on April 9, Austin officials approved more than $50 million for local affordable housing initiatives, including $29.27 million for existing and planned developments. On April 12, City Council approved a new development near West Campus. And on March 26, Council approved the Montopolis Saxon Lane property transfer (0.09-acre city-owned site) for affordable housing development — enabled by HOME Initiative lot size reductions.

Staff will spend the next year developing missing middle draft ordinances, with completion expected by March 2027.

**May 17 update:** Council received a detailed work session briefing on the Citywide Density Bonus Program on May 5. Staff proposed five new bonus tiers ranging from no additional height to up to 60 additional feet beyond base zoning, responding to SB 840 requirements. SB 840 ('residential in commercial') allows unlimited FAR and up to 54 units/acre by right in commercial zones — undermining the city's traditional density bonus leverage for affordable housing. More than 20% of Austin's income-restricted housing was created through density bonus programs now affected by SB 840. The council vote on the revised DBC is expected May 28 (9 DAYS). Meanwhile, Council voted 9-1 on May 8 to push the HOME Initiative deeper into single-family neighborhoods — loosening lot-size, setback, and subdivision rules for missing middle housing. Over 600 HOME applications have been approved, adding 1,200+ new housing units. CM Duchen cast the lone no vote, citing concerns about luxury housing displacing lower-cost existing homes. Community Not Commodity published a detailed critique (May 11) arguing the DBC proposal would allow 90-foot buildings 25 feet from single-family lot lines and 120-foot buildings 50 feet from lot lines — expect organized opposition at the May 28 council vote.

**May 19 update:** The DBC council vote date has been clarified: Austin City Council has a regular meeting on May 21 where the DBC may be considered, though the full council vote is still expected May 28 (9 DAYS). Community Not Commodity's detailed opposition piece (May 11) continues to circulate — expect organized opposition at the council vote.

**May 20 update:** ⚡ THE DBC COUNCIL VOTE IS TOMORROW (MAY 21). Austin City Council's regular meeting agenda for May 21 includes a PUBLIC HEARING AND CONSIDERATION of an ordinance creating the new citywide density bonus zoning districts, replacing DB90 and VMU. This is the vote — not May 28 as previously expected. The ordinance would amend City Code Title 25 to create new zoning districts allowing additional height (including more than 30 feet above base) in exchange for affordable housing and community benefits, applicable to commercial and multifamily base zones citywide. Community Not Commodity's opposition campaign continues to circulate. Show up or submit written testimony TODAY.

public_comment.instructions: Community engagement ongoing through 2026-2027. Monitor austintexas.gov/development-services/home-amendments for public hearing schedules.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: This is HOME Initiative Phase 3 in everything but name. The missing middle zoning categories create purpose-built pathways for the gentle density that makes neighborhoods walkable — townhomes, cottage courts, small mixed-use buildings. This is especially relevant for 78702 where transit-oriented development along the future light rail corridor needs exactly this kind of zoning. ⚡ THE DBC PUBLIC HEARING IS TOMORROW (May 21) — submit testimony or show up at council.

issues.title: Front Yard Businesses — Council Approves Micro-Business Pilot
issues.summary: NEW (April 2, 2026): Austin City Council approved the 'Strong Local Commerce Initiative,' led by CM Harper-Madison, allowing small businesses to operate visibly from residential properties. The resolution permits front yard stands, artisanal goods sales, service windows for small-scale repair, garden stands, and accessory retail units up to 200 sq ft on residential properties. Features include closer placement to sidewalks and 'modest' signage. A 12-month neighborhood storefront pilot program will test the concept before permanent changes. Some residents raised concerns about noise, traffic, and congestion in residential areas.

public_comment.instructions: Resolution passed. Monitor the 12-month pilot program for outcomes and potential permanent adoption.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: This aligns with the urbanist agenda — decentralizing economic opportunity and increasing walkability. Allowing visible front-yard businesses is a small but meaningful step toward the kind of mixed-use neighborhood fabric that makes density livable. The pilot approach is pragmatic. Worth monitoring for how it interacts with HOME Initiative density increases.

issues.title: 2026 Bond Package — BEATF Proposes $766.5M, Council Deliberating
issues.summary:
⚡ MAJOR UPDATE (May 6, 2026): The Bond Election Advisory Task Force (BEATF) has released its formal proposals — TWO OPTIONS for council consideration:

⚡ PRIMARY PROPOSAL: $766.53 MILLION (passed UNANIMOUSLY by the task force):
• Affordable housing: $200M
• Parks and recreation: $175M
• Transportation (sidewalks, Vision Zero, trails): $147M
• Public facilities (community centers, animal shelter, homeless services): $131.1M
• Drainage and flood prevention: $113.43M

⚡ ALTERNATIVE PROPOSAL: ~$440 MILLION (passed with abstentions and opposing votes):
• Parks, mobility, and public facilities only
• EXCLUDES housing and watershed protection

TAX IMPACT: The $766.5M package would increase median homeowner property taxes by approximately $100/year. The smaller option would cost roughly $60/year.

Task force member Tina Cannon opposed the council-requested smaller option, stating the city faces 'over $10 billion in unmet needs.' Members emphasized East Austin priorities, particularly the George Washington Carver Museum and Colony Park library improvements.

Some officials suggested pursuing the smaller package in 2026 and a larger bond in 2028 when debt capacity improves. City budget staff had recommended a cap of $687M based on debt capacity.

BUDGET CONTEXT (April 22): FY2027 budget talks have begun in earnest. The general fund is expected to grow only ~3%, with no pay raises planned for civilian city employees. Some council members said an early priority is maintaining the nearly $9M in affordable housing funding set to be eliminated. Budget work sessions set to begin in July with adoption expected in August.

⚡ TIMELINE: BEATF will present its formal recommendation to City Council on MAY 28 — 9 DAYS AWAY. Council will then define the final bond package and call an election in August. The fiscal pressure is acute — voters rejected Proposition Q last November, which would have generated $110M for parks and homelessness services. That rejection, combined with the FY2027 $26.4M projected deficit, means the bond package is the primary remaining mechanism for large-scale capital investment.

**May 17 update:** BEATF finalized two proposals: the full $766.53M package and a narrower $436M alternative (excluding housing). The main package includes $200M for affordable housing, $175M for parkland, $147M for transportation, $131.1M for public facilities (including Colony Park community center and new homeless shelter), and $113.43M for drainage/flood prevention. The full package exceeds the $700M cap recommended by city finance staff. Late BEATF discussions (May 12-14) focused on East Austin priorities — members agreed to reduce some park, housing, and transportation allocations to fully fund the George Washington Carver Museum and Colony Park library/health center. The task force also recommended a 'contract with the voters' requiring clear project timelines and more participation from other local governments in future bond processes. BEATF presents formal recommendations to Council on May 28 — 9 DAYS AWAY. Council will then define the final bond package and call an election in August.

**May 19 update:** BEATF presentation to Council on May 28 is now 9 DAYS AWAY. No new developments since May 17.

**May 20 update:** ⚡ BOMBSHELL: KXAN reports that city financial staff have recommended DELAYING the bond election to 2028, not 2026. Capital Delivery Services Director Adriana Castaneda said staff recommend postponement so the city can stay within current financial policies — city rules generally bar holding another bond election until two years or less remain on previously authorized bonds. Moving forward with a 2026 bond would require council to change the city's financial rules. However, in a work session, many council members expressed they STILL want to move forward with a bond this year, especially for parks. This creates a direct tension between staff fiscal guidance and council political priorities. BEATF presentation to Council is May 28 (8 DAYS). The staff recommendation adds a major new variable to the bond debate.

public_comment.instructions: Public engagement ongoing at speakupaustin.org. BEATF recommendations due May 28. Council vote to place on ballot expected summer 2026.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: The BEATF's $766.5M primary proposal passed UNANIMOUSLY — a strong signal of consensus. The $200M affordable housing allocation is the centerpiece for pro-housing advocates. The alternative $440M package that excludes housing was controversial within the task force. After Prop Q's defeat, this bond is the primary remaining mechanism for large-scale public investment in housing, transit, and infrastructure. BEATF presents to Council on May 28 — 8 DAYS AWAY. ⚡ NEW TWIST: City financial staff recommend delaying the bond to 2028, but council members pushed back — creating tension between fiscal caution and political priorities. The task force's 'contract with the voters' recommendation is a smart political move — it addresses voter skepticism about bond accountability that contributed to Prop Q's defeat. The key question is whether council goes with the full $766.5M or the stripped-down $440M. The tax impact ($100/year for the full package) will be the political battleground. Engage through SpeakUpAustin to support the full package including the $200M housing allocation.

issues.title: Waymo AV Blocks Ambulance — Committee Held Meeting Without Waymo
issues.summary:
UPDATE (April 26, 2026): A Waymo autonomous vehicle blocked an ambulance responding to the mass shooting at Buford's on West 6th Street on March 1. Video showed the driverless car stopped across both lanes; an APD officer had to manually move it. ATCEMS Chief Luckritz said the incident didn't affect overall response but it was 'unacceptable' that personnel had to divert attention during an active emergency. Five council members (Qadri, Vela, Velasquez, Ellis, Laine) signed a letter inviting Waymo to a joint Public Safety and Mobility Committee meeting on April 29 to discuss coordination with emergency services.

⚡ APRIL 25: WAYMO DECLINED the council invitation, saying it had 'already had substantive conversations directly with all five requesting Council offices, more than 15 city officials, and the highest levels of state government.'

⚡ APRIL 29 — MEETING HELD WITHOUT WAYMO: The joint Public Safety and Mobility Committee proceeded as scheduled (11am-1pm) without Waymo representation. Council members heard testimony from ATCEMS, APD, and members of the public regarding autonomous vehicle safety coordination with emergency services. CM Qadri reiterated: 'Their absence speaks for itself.' Committee members discussed potential regulatory options including requiring AV operators to coordinate directly with emergency dispatch and establishing emergency vehicle right-of-way protocols for autonomous vehicles. NEXT STEPS: Council staff directed to research municipal regulatory authority over AV operations and report back at a future council session. Staff has recommended a 1,000-FOOT AV EXCLUSION ZONE around active emergency scenes. Additionally, the city is pushing for new state driverless vehicle legislation — new state DMV enforcement authority takes effect May 28.

CONTEXT: Council also passed the TRUST Act on April 24, establishing a new regulatory framework for surveillance technology used by city departments. The ordinance requires council approval before deploying new data-collection technology, bans facial recognition, and limits how collected data can be used. The TRUST Act reflects a broader council posture toward technology accountability — the same impulse driving the Waymo hearing.

**May 17 update:** Waymo issued a national recall of 3,791 robotaxis after a vehicle was swept away during San Antonio flash flooding on April 20. The over-the-air software fix addresses the vehicle's failure to stop on higher-speed roadways when detecting potentially flooded lanes. ⚡ KEY DATE: A new Texas law takes effect May 28 (8 DAYS) requiring AV companies to obtain Texas DMV authorization before operating commercially; the DMV can now restrict operators whose vehicles cause serious bodily injury. Austin has two representatives on the new Automated Vehicle Regulation Advisory Committee. Waymo's Austin fleet has grown to approximately 300 vehicles. Council staff's recommended 1,000-foot AV exclusion zone around active emergency scenes is still pending review.

public_comment.instructions: Joint Public Safety and Mobility Committee meeting held April 29 without Waymo. Monitor council for follow-up regulatory actions.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: The April 29 meeting proceeded without Waymo — confirming the company's refusal to engage directly with elected officials on a public safety matter. Council members are now exploring regulatory options, including emergency vehicle right-of-way protocols for AVs and mandatory coordination with dispatch. Combined with the TRUST Act (April 24), Austin is establishing a clear framework: technology companies operating in public spaces must be accountable to the communities they serve. Watch for the staff report on municipal regulatory authority — this could set precedent for how cities nationwide handle AV safety oversight.

issues.title: Project Connect — TX Supreme Court Still Pending, $230M Land Acquisition Approved
issues.governing_body: Austin Transit Partnership / Texas Supreme Court
issues.summary:
UPDATE (May 8, 2026): The Texas Supreme Court has STILL NOT ruled on AG Paxton's challenge to Project Connect's funding mechanism — now 99 DAYS since oral arguments on February 10. The procedural question remains: whether the AG's office was allowed to file an early appeal. Meanwhile, the project continues advancing:

• January 2026: Federal environmental review (Record of Decision) signed off by Trump administration — a critical milestone for ~$4 billion in FTA Capital Investment Grant funding.
• February 18, 2026: Austin Transit Partnership approved a $60 million design-build contract with Austin Rail Constructors for Phase 1.
⚡ APRIL 15: ATP approved a $25 MILLION contract with Kiewit Austin Partnership for the Phase 1 operations and maintenance facility. ATP also approved spending up to $230 MILLION to acquire up to 18 parcels for the rail corridor and maintenance facility — the first major land acquisition push. Mayor Watson noted this is 'the beginning of the process where people may anticipate condemnation actions and eminent domain actions.' ATP will negotiate initially; the city handles eminent domain if needed (12-18 month process).
• Early field work continues along the corridor. Full construction expected to begin 2027. Target completion: 2033.

APRIL 13: Mayor Watson blocked ATP's proposed $47 million downtown office deal ($32M lease + $15M buildout for 51,600 sq ft at Congress & Cesar Chavez), calling it 'inappropriate' given the city's budget challenges. Watson pushed ATP to co-locate with CapMetro. The vote was postponed indefinitely — signals political pressure on ATP to demonstrate spending restraint while the legal cloud persists.

The legal uncertainty remains the single biggest risk: if the court sides with Paxton, the entire $8.23 billion project — and its $166M/year dedicated tax revenue — could be jeopardized. But the continued advancement of contracts, land acquisition, and federal milestones signals confidence from ATP leadership.

As of May 19, the court has gone 99 DAYS since oral arguments with no ruling — an unusually long wait suggesting the justices may be divided or drafting a complex opinion.

**May 17 update:** The Texas Supreme Court ruling remains pending — now 96 DAYS since oral arguments on February 10 with no decision, an unusually long wait. ⚡ CRITICAL: A Travis County District Court trial is scheduled for May 28-30 (11 DAYS) to weigh the legality of Project Connect's funding structure — this trial runs CONCURRENTLY with the BEATF bond presentation to council (also May 28). CM Paige Ellis called ATP's planned $32M office lease at 100 Congress Ave 'politically tone-deaf'; ATP pulled the item and is exploring co-location with CapMetro. Construction planned to start 2027, operational by 2033. ATP expects to award three major contracts in 2026: the full route and 15 stations, the operations/maintenance facility at US-183/SH-71, and the train cars.

**May 19 update:** TX Supreme Court ruling now 99 DAYS since oral arguments — still no decision. The Travis County District Court trial is May 28-30 — 8 DAYS AWAY. No new developments since May 17.

public_comment.instructions: Monitor TX Supreme Court docket for ruling. Support Project Connect through Austin Transit Partnership community engagement.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Project Connect is the single largest infrastructure investment in Austin's history and the backbone of the transit-oriented development strategy. The $60M contract award and federal environmental clearance are major wins, but the TX Supreme Court ruling hangs over everything — 99 days and counting since oral arguments with no ruling. When the ruling comes, organized pro-transit response will be critical — whether celebrating a win or mobilizing against a loss. This remains the highest-stakes item on Austin's civic agenda. ⚡ The May 28-30 trial in Travis County (8 DAYS) adds a new front — even if the Supreme Court rules favorably on the procedural question, the underlying funding legality will be tested at trial.

issues.title: COTA $985M Resort Project — Council Approves Incentive Deal
issues.summary:
UPDATE (April 26, 2026): Austin City Council APPROVED the incentive deal on April 23 for a $985 million hotel and convention center project at Circuit of the Americas. Houston-based RIDA Development Corporation will build a 1,000-room hotel and 170,000-square-foot conference center adjacent to the Formula 1 track. The deal is expected to create about 900 permanent full-time hospitality, food service, maintenance, and management jobs, plus ~4,000 temporary construction jobs.

The incentive structure is performance-based: Austin will rebate a portion of hotel occupancy tax revenue (capped at 8.25% of gross room night revenue) over up to 30 years, with payments not beginning until the hotel is operational and generating tax revenue. Officials estimate the project could generate roughly $200 million in property and sales tax revenue over three decades. The deal breaks down roughly 89% private / 11% public investment — city staff noted 'anything 15% or below is considered a good use of public dollars.' Council also advanced a zoning update on April 23, with final PUD approval expected late May.

TIMELINE: RIDA anticipates an 18-month entitlement process followed by 30-36 months of construction. The resort will include dining, a golf course, driving range, and a lazy river similar to RIDA's Marriott Marquis Houston.

RIDA reached a labor peace agreement with Unite Here Local 23. Council also approved a land title transfer required under state law for the project to qualify for state hotel occupancy and sales tax rebates.

The resort is designed to serve mid-sized events and complement — not compete with — Austin's new downtown convention center currently under construction ($1.6B, opening late 2028).

public_comment.instructions: Deal approved April 23. Monitor implementation and hotel construction timeline.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Deal done. A $985M private investment with only 11% public exposure and 900 permanent jobs is a favorable outcome by city staff metrics. The performance-based structure (no payments until the hotel is generating revenue) mitigates risk. $200M in projected property/sales tax revenue over 30 years strengthens Austin's fiscal position — relevant context as the city navigates a $26.4M FY2027 deficit. This doesn't directly advance housing supply, but fiscal health supports everything else.

issues.title: AISD $181M Budget Deficit — Budget Meetings Continue Through June
issues.summary:
⚡ UPDATE (May 8, 2026): TODAY — campuses begin receiving staffing allocations (May 8-11). Leadership meets with potentially affected staff May 12-15.

⚡ MAY 6 BOMBSHELL (KUT): AISD proposes LARGER CLASS SIZES and CUTS TO TEACHER PLANNING TIME at most campuses. The preliminary budget spares Band 1 campuses (all TAP campuses and the most vulnerable schools per the Support and Resource Index), but ALL OTHER CAMPUSES face increased class sizes and reduced planning periods. Campus-level changes will phase in over the next two school years.

⚡ CUTS IDENTIFIED:
• Central & department budgets: $73.8M in reductions, including eliminating the Deputy Superintendent of Business and Operations vacancy and other central office positions
• Campus budgets: $33.9M in proposed cuts — now confirmed to include larger class sizes
• Revenue generation: $45M from real estate monetization
• 10 campus closures: Expected to save $21M, with $17M reinvested into school improvements

⚡ POSITIONS PROTECTED:
• Librarians and counselors will NOT be eliminated
• Teacher planning time and class sizes PROTECTED ONLY at turnaround/unacceptable-rated campuses (Band 1)
• Bilingual and special education stipends prioritized

⚡ CRITICAL TIMELINE:
• ⚡ May 8-11: Campuses receive staffing allocations (HAPPENING NOW)
• May 9: Virtual budget meeting, 11am-noon
• May 12-15: Leadership meets with potentially affected staff
• May 21: Recommended Budget presented to Board
• June 18: Final board vote on budget
• By June 19: Notifications sent to impacted employees

BACKGROUND: The district faces a $181M FY2027 deficit. Current-year deficit: $49M. The 10 campus closures already approved for 2026-27 will proceed, but Superintendent Segura paused additional closures (April 24). Primary drivers: declining enrollment (3,000+ students lost — each worth $14,889 in per-pupil funding), Austin property values down 18% from 2022 peak, and Texas 'Robin Hood' school finance recapture.

**May 17 update:** AISD confirmed 215 full-time educator position cuts — 85 elementary, 51 middle school, 79 high school — for 2026-27. Staffing allocations went to campuses May 8-11; leadership met with potentially affected staff May 12-15. Central and department reductions total $73.8M. ⚡ NEXT: The recommended budget is presented to the Board on May 21 — 4 DAYS AWAY. Final Board of Trustees vote: June 18. The district has lost nearly 3,200 students from last school year, compounding the deficit alongside decreased property values and a delayed $26M property sale. The larger class sizes and cuts to teacher planning time (May 6 KUT report) are now being implemented at all non-Band 1 campuses. Superintendent Segura clarified that not all 215 positions will result in layoffs — hundreds of vacancies exist — but teacher-student ratios will increase at most campuses.

**May 19 update:** ⚡ The recommended budget presentation to the Board is TOMORROW (May 21). KVUE reported under the headline 'There are no good options' on the preliminary budget outlook. Austin Chronicle reported AISD proposes cutting over 200 full-time positions. The 215 confirmed educator position cuts (85 elementary, 51 middle school, 79 high school) and larger class sizes at non-Band 1 campuses remain the core of the budget plan. Final Board vote: June 18.

public_comment.instructions: AISD board meetings allow public comment. Next: May 9 virtual budget meeting, 11am-noon. May 21 recommended budget. Final vote June 18. Staffing allocations go to campuses May 8-11.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: ⚡ The recommended budget presentation is TOMORROW (May 21). KVUE's 'There are no good options' headline and the Austin Chronicle's report on 200+ position cuts underscore the severity. The May 6 KUT report confirms AISD is proposing larger class sizes and cuts to teacher planning time at all but the most struggling schools. Only Band 1 campuses (turnaround/unacceptable-rated) are protected. This is the point where the budget crisis becomes real for families. The AISD crisis remains directly connected to the housing fight. Declining enrollment (3,000+ students lost) reflects families leaving urban Austin — larger class sizes accelerate the flight. Supporting school quality IS pro-housing policy. May 21 recommended budget (TOMORROW), June 18 final vote.

issues.title: Austin Backs Down on ICE Policy — APD Updating General Orders to Cooperate
issues.summary:
UPDATE (April 26, 2026): Austin is backing down. On April 24, APD announced it will UPDATE ITS GENERAL ORDERS regarding cooperation with ICE and immigration operations, following Governor Abbott's threat to pull $2.5 million in state public safety grants. The Governor's Public Safety Office granted Austin an extension after what the city described as 'good faith discussions.' Abbott expects any revised policy to require 'full cooperation with DHS, including notifying DHS and detaining individuals subject to removal proceedings.'

This follows the same pattern as Houston (changed its ordinance April 22) and Dallas (revised its ICE policy April 23-24). All three major Texas cities have now yielded to the state's fiscal pressure.

BACKGROUND: The dispute centered on Austin City Council's March 4 revisions to policies governing how APD officers work with Immigration and Customs Enforcement. Under the revised policy, officers were NOT authorized to make arrests or detentions based solely on ICE administrative warrants. The Governor's Public Safety Office demanded repeal or face termination of all public safety grants.

The $2.5M at stake funds public safety programs. In a city facing a $26.4M FY2027 deficit with $16.8M in proposed social services cuts, the fiscal pressure proved decisive. Unlike the 2017 'sanctuary city' fight (when Austin absorbed the consequences), the 2026 fiscal environment left no room to hold the line.

public_comment.instructions: Monitor council response to the deadline. The next council meeting will likely address this issue.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Austin joined Houston and Dallas in yielding to Abbott's fiscal pressure. The $2.5M grant preservation is pragmatically defensible — with 340 APD vacancies and a $26.4M deficit, losing additional public safety funding was untenable. But the pattern matters: all three major Texas cities backed down within a week, demonstrating that state-level fiscal leverage can override local policy preferences. This is relevant context as five council seats turn over in November — candidates' positions on state-vs-local authority and immigrant community trust will matter. The policy reversal may also affect AISD enrollment trends, since immigration enforcement fears were identified as a driver of the 3,000+ student decline.

issues.title: TRUST Act Passed — Austin Regulates Surveillance Technology
issues.summary:
NEW (April 24, 2026): Austin City Council passed the Transparent and Responsible Use of Surveillance Technology Act (TRUST Act), establishing a new regulatory framework for how city departments use surveillance technology.

Key provisions:
• City departments must obtain council approval before deploying new technology that collects data on residents
• Departments must disclose how data is collected, stored, and shared
• Facial recognition technology is BANNED
• Data collection for marketing purposes is prohibited
• Publicly available policies required describing each use of surveillance technology

The ordinance follows a February 2026 council resolution directing the city manager to draft the regulation, which itself followed the June 2025 decision to end Austin's contract with Flock Safety (automatic license plate readers) after community privacy concerns. Council members added amendments during the April 24 vote.

This positions Austin among the most restrictive US cities on government surveillance technology. The TRUST Act reflects growing tension between technology deployment for public safety and civil liberties protections.

public_comment.instructions: Ordinance passed. Monitor implementation and any department technology approval requests that come to council.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: The TRUST Act is a significant win for privacy and government transparency — and it connects to the Waymo conversation happening April 29. Austin is establishing a framework for how the city interacts with emerging technology, from surveillance cameras to autonomous vehicles. The facial recognition ban and data disclosure requirements set a high bar that will shape future technology debates. For urbanists, this matters because technology deployment in public spaces affects the livability of dense neighborhoods. Worth monitoring how the ordinance interacts with APD's staffing challenges — if departments can't use certain surveillance tools, it strengthens the case for adequate human staffing.

issues.title: Texas House Passes Historic $100M Childcare Investment (HB 500)
issues.summary:
NEW (May 2026): The Texas House approved HB 500, a supplemental budget bill containing $100 MILLION for child care scholarships. The funding allows the Texas Workforce Commission to serve additional families in its Child Care Services program — critical given that 95,000 families are currently on the waiting list.

This builds on Governor Abbott's earlier signing of HB 117 and HB 4903 — HB 117 streamlines childcare regulations, while HB 4903 creates the Quad-Agency Childcare Initiative Commission to coordinate child care regulation across multiple state agencies.

LOCAL CONTEXT: Austin/Travis County's 'Raising Travis County' voter-approved initiative is in full rollout in 2026 — the fastest implementation of any childcare initiative of its kind in the country and the first in Texas. The $5M county transfer (March 2026) funds an interlocal agreement with the City of Austin for childcare quality improvement. Combined with the state's $100M investment, this creates a rare alignment of city, county, and state childcare funding.

The failure to provide adequate child care costs Texas $11.4 billion annually from productivity and revenue losses.

public_comment.instructions: State legislation. Monitor Texas Workforce Commission for implementation timeline and how Austin/Travis County families can access the new scholarships.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: A rare bipartisan win — $100M for childcare scholarships addresses a critical gap for Austin families. The 95,000-family waitlist is a concrete measure of the crisis. Combined with Raising Travis County's local investment, Austin is now at the intersection of city, county, AND state childcare funding. This creates an opportunity for the Abundance Project agenda: mapping where the new scholarship dollars flow, identifying remaining childcare deserts, and advocating for employer-side solutions to complement public investment.

issues.title: Economic Development Playbook — Council Resolution
issues.summary: On May 7, Council passed a Mayor Watson resolution directing creation of an 'active, progressive' economic development playbook. The framework will set minimum standards and compliance mandates for community benefits attached to future development agreements, recruit new investment, attract international projects, and support local businesses. Watson stated: 'This framework makes Austin's values binding, visible and consistently applied so that every economic decision reflects who we are.' The initiative was supported by Austin's business community and chambers of commerce, and is partly driven by the need to address the city's strained finances by expanding its revenue base amid increased suburban competition for corporate relocations.
issues.why_it_matters_to_you: A formalized economic development playbook could shape which businesses and industries Austin recruits, with community benefit requirements that align with progressive values around equity and affordability. The framework's emphasis on binding community standards could ensure future development deals contribute to affordable housing and equitable growth.

issues.title: Light Rail Transit Overlay Adopted — Transit-Supportive Zoning Along Phase 1 Corridor
issues.summary: NEW (May 16, 2026): Austin City Council adopted the Light Rail Transit Overlay (Ordinance No. 20260516-005) to increase transit-supportive land uses and encourage affordable housing near the Phase 1 Austin Light Rail corridor and its Priority Extensions. The overlay creates new zoning incentives for dense, mixed-use development within walking distance of the 15 planned light rail stations stretching from 38th Street to Oltorf and east from Downtown to East Riverside. This is a critical companion policy to the Project Connect infrastructure investment — ensuring that the $8.23 billion transit system catalyzes housing production and walkable neighborhoods rather than remaining surrounded by parking lots and single-family-only zoning. The overlay responds to national evidence that transit investments without supportive land use policy fail to generate ridership or housing affordability benefits.

public_comment.instructions: Overlay adopted. Monitor implementation and specific station-area plans as they develop.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: This is the land use policy that makes Project Connect's transit investment pay off. Without transit-supportive zoning, light rail stations get built next to parking lots. The overlay ensures density, mixed-use, and affordable housing incentives around all 15 stations — directly in your 78702 backyard along the East Riverside corridor. Combined with the Density Bonus Program (Council vote May 28), this creates a comprehensive framework for transit-oriented development. This is YIMBY policy at its best: using public infrastructure investment to unlock private housing production.

issues.title: 12 Shootings Across Austin May 17 — Public Safety Strain Exposed
issues.summary: NEW (May 17, 2026): At least 12 shooting incidents occurred across Austin on May 17, leaving four people wounded. Two teenage suspects and a third suspect were taken into custody. The incidents spanned multiple neighborhoods and overwhelmed police response capacity. APD released a detailed timeline on May 18, identifying three suspects: Cristian Fajardo Mondragon (17), and two juveniles aged 16 and 15. The incidents were a mix of 'random and targeted shootings.' Investigators connected the cases through surveillance footage, ballistic evidence, stolen vehicle reporting, and coordinated intelligence sharing. APD confirmed there is no longer an ongoing threat to the public. This follows the March 1 mass shooting on Sixth Street that killed three and injured more than a dozen, and which the FBI investigated as potential terrorism. The spate of shootings underscores the ongoing strain on APD, which has 340 vacancies out of 1,816 authorized positions (19% vacancy rate). Despite recent progress — APD met its Priority 0/1 response time targets for the first time since August 2023 — the May 17 incidents demonstrate that recovery is fragile. The 157th cadet class (65 cadets) began April 21, but full staffing isn't expected until end of 2027.

public_comment.instructions: Monitor APD and council response. FY2027 budget hearings (July) will address public safety staffing and funding.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Twelve shootings in a single day is a visceral reminder that Austin's public safety recovery is incomplete. The 340 APD vacancies and the March 1 mass shooting already framed this issue — May 17 adds urgency. This will likely dominate the 2026 council elections, with candidates in all five open seats forced to articulate positions on policing, gun violence prevention, and the hospital-based violence prevention programs. The budget context makes it harder: with a $26.4M FY2027 deficit, expanding public safety capacity competes directly with housing, homelessness, and infrastructure investments.

issues.title: 6 Affordable Housing Proposals Receive Council Support for LIHTC Funding
issues.summary: NEW (May 2026): Austin City Council provided resolutions of support for six affordable housing proposals seeking competitive Low Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC) funding from the Texas Department of Housing and Community Affairs. LIHTC is the primary federal tool for financing affordable housing construction. Council support resolutions are required for competitive applications and signal local government backing. This comes alongside the $46.1 million approved to build and preserve affordable housing in Austin, spanning both new construction and rehabilitation of existing units. The LIHTC pipeline is critical infrastructure for the city's affordable housing goals — these six projects would add hundreds of income-restricted units across Austin.

public_comment.instructions: Resolutions of support passed. Monitor TDHCA for LIHTC award decisions (typically fall 2026).

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: LIHTC is the workhorse of affordable housing finance — these six council-backed projects represent the pipeline of income-restricted housing that complements the market-rate supply expansion driven by HOME Initiative reforms. The $46.1M in approved funding plus the LIHTC pipeline demonstrates Austin's multi-pronged approach to affordability: build more market-rate housing (HOME) while simultaneously investing in income-restricted units (LIHTC, AHFC). Track TDHCA award decisions this fall to see which projects move forward.
